title: GDPR Binding Corporate Rules (BCR) Evidence and Intra-Group Transfer Monitoring

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.47 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.47 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.40: GDPR Binding Corporate Rules (BCR) Evidence and Intra-Group Transfer Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.40' running on gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv, CIM Network_Traffic, Active Directory or HR entity metadata, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv`, CIM Network_Traffic, Active Directory or HR entity metadata
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where earliest=-30d match(All_Traffic.src_category,"(?i)internal")
  by All_Traffic.src All_Traffic.dest
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| lookup gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv entity_id AS src OUTPUT bcr_covered, entity_name
| lookup gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv entity_id AS dest OUTPUT bcr_covered AS dest_bcr, entity_name AS dest_name
| eval bcr_gap=if(bcr_covered="true" AND dest_bcr!="true", "FLOW_TO_NON_BCR", "review")
| where bcr_gap="FLOW_TO_NON_BCR"
| table src, dest, entity_name, dest_name, bytes_out, bcr_gap
description: BCRs require demonstrable enforcement of group-wide policies including transfers between entities. This use case maps intra-group data flows to BCR-covered entities, verifies annual certification of policy acknowledgement, and detects flows involving non-BCR entities.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Assign `entity_id` to sites and cloud subscriptions in `gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv`; (2) tag internal IP ranges and cloud accounts; (3) alert when significant volumes flow to entities not listed as BCR members; (4) join HR training data for annual BCR attestation; (5) include in group privacy committee reporting.
visualization: Sankey (intra-group flows), Table (non-BCR recipients), Single value (policy acknowledgement %), Bar chart (volume by entity). Key fields: `bcr_gap`, `bytes_out`, `dest`, `dest_name`, `entity_name`, `src`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
knownFalsePositives:
1. **BCR approval processing time** — BCR applications submitted to the lead supervisory authority may take 12-18 months for approval; transfers under interim safeguards during this period are documented.

2. **BCR scope amendments** — Changes to group structure (new entities, divestitures) requiring BCR amendments may show gaps while amendments are being processed; check the amendment application status.

3. **Intra-group entity name changes** — Corporate restructuring that changes entity names without changing processing relationships may trigger BCR mapping alerts.

4. **BCR annual compliance report timing** — The annual BCR compliance report to the supervisory authority may show temporary non-conformities being addressed as part of continuous improvement.

5. **BCR training rollout phases** — New group entities being integrated into BCR training programmes may show compliance gaps during the onboarding period documented in the BCR implementation plan.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv`, `gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv','gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv')`.
• **Data Model Acceleration**: The CIM datamodel referenced in the search must be accelerated. Validate with `| rest /services/admin/summarization | search summary.id=*` and confirm `summary.complete=100`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.47`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.47 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.40: GDPR Binding Corporate Rules (BCR) Evidence and Intra-Group Transfer Monitoring." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Assign `entity_id` to sites and cloud subscriptions in `gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv`. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 2:** tag internal IP ranges and cloud accounts. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 3:** alert when significant volumes flow to entities not listed as BCR members. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** join HR training data for annual BCR attestation. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** include in group privacy committee reporting. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Binding Corporate Rules (BCR) Evidence and Intra-Group Transfer Monitoring," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of GDPR control activity.

This search uses `| tstats` against a CIM-accelerated data model rather than reading raw events. The performance delta is typically 10-50x over raw search, and for large indexes can be >100x. The acceleration summaries are maintained on a rolling window (default: 1 year, configurable in the data model editor) and are queried from indexer-local summary files rather than re-scanning raw events.

CIM acceleration dependency: this search FAILS SILENTLY if the underlying data model is not accelerated. It will return zero rows instead of raising an error, which is exactly the wrong failure mode for a compliance detection. Verify acceleration status before scheduling:

```spl
| rest /services/admin/summarization
| search title="<datamodel_name>"
| table title access_count summary.complete summary.earliest_time summary.latest_time
```

`summary.complete` should show 100 (or very close). If it's below 95, the acceleration is still catching up or has stalled — check the DMA audit log (`index=_audit action=dma_run`) for errors.

Aggregation choice: `sum()` is grouped by `All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest`. This produces a summary row per distinct combination, which is typically what compliance reporting requires (one row per asset, one row per user, one row per rule). If downstream processing expects a flat event stream, avoid `tstats` — summary rows are not raw events and cannot be drilled into without re-running against the raw index.

When NOT to use tstats: if the field being filtered was added after the acceleration started, or is search-time extracted by an app the acceleration isn't aware of, the field will not exist in the summaries and the `where` clause will drop every row. Rebuild the acceleration after any field-extraction changes.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Network_Traffic` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Network_Traffic` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.40: GDPR Binding Corporate Rules (BCR) Evidence and Intra-Group ]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.40,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `bcr_gap`, `bytes_out`, `dest`, `dest_name`, `entity_name`, `src`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table bcr_gap, bytes_out, dest, dest_name, entity_name, src
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_bcr_entity_register.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.40: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Sankey (`intra-group flows`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (`non-BCR recipients`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`policy acknowledgement %`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 4 — Distribution (`volume by entity`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Zero results when acceleration is fresh:** The field you're filtering on was likely added after the acceleration started — rebuild the acceleration.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We bCRs require demonstrable enforcement of group-wide policies including transfers between entities so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Add a new group entity to the BCR scope without updating the BCR member register; verify the intra-group transfer monitoring detects transfers to the unregistered entity and flags them as potentially uncovered.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Add a new group entity to the BCR scope with proper registration in the member list and compliance training evidence; verify intra-group transfers to the new entity appear as covered.

evidence: BCR compliance evidence pack showing member entity coverage, annual compliance report summary, training completion rates, and binding corporate rules update history.
exclusions: Non-personal data transfers between group entities; transfers to entities covered by alternative safeguards (SCCs) pending BCR scope amendment processing.
